speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 26, 1996. This is an interview with David Jacobs. Name at birth, Jakubowicz. I'm the interviewer, Allen Charney. We're in Toronto, Canada, and we'll conduct the interview in English. The e is may 26, 1996. This is an interview with David Jacobs, first name spelled D-A-V-I-D. Last name, J-A-C-O-B-S, that's J-A-C-O-B-S. Name at birth, surname at birth, Jakubowicz. J-A-K-U-B-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am the interviewer, Allen Charney, first name A-L-L-E-N. Last name Charney, C-H-A-R-N-E-Y. We are in Toronto, Canada. And we'll conduct the interview in English. Mr Jacobs, state your name please.

speaker: interviewee
text: David Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-C-O-B-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name would be spelled--

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-A-V-I-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family name at birth was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakubowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-K-U-O-B-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to go back to that spelling. Can you spell it one more time, Jakubowicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. J-A-K-U-B-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names in Poland, prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were known by, Hebrew names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dovid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth is what?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1918, the 28th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow Mazowiecki. Hits

speaker: interviewer
text: What country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell Tomaszow Mazowiecki.

speaker: interviewee
text: T-O-M-A-S-Z-O-W M-A-Z. That spells Mazowiecki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those are two separate words, Tomaszow Mazowiecki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age, sir, is-- you are how old now?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a tailor. And a nice person, in one word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born nine kilometers from Tomaszow, that's called Ujazd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately what was his age, let's say in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was about-- I think about 56, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about his business.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very successful. We had a lot of customers, as a matter of fact, German customers. There had been a lot of German in Tomaszow, living there before the war. And they were very good customers. And we did work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work independently? Or did he have people that worked for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we have people what did work for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many employees did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about four people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship to your father? What's your most distinct memory of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very good relationship. Because my father was orthodox. And sometimes he looked away things. He always liked to see the kids. They're going with him to the synagogue, which did make him happy. And later on, we were on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that he always used to look away. Give me an example of him looking away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not always look away. He didn't like to, but accordingly, the younger generation, they had their mind on their own. They liked to go to a club, where is sporting. And that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say he would look away, explain what you mean by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That he knew that we went away to some- that on Saturday, which is a Jewish religion holy day, instead of going to the synagogue after lunch, we went away, as young kids, we tried to play with the other kids and go--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you be doing instead of going to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went, we played soccer, we played ping pong. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any disagreements with your father about things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. As long he didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hana Feiger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michlewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know the place and date of her birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The only thing I know, she was about between five and six years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was about-- you had said--

speaker: interviewee
text: When they start to get rid of every Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tomaszow. Like they send them out to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father would have been 56 in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940-- between 1942 and 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother would have been how old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She would be about-- between 48 and 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, she was a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she always, the kids, the kids. And she did look so much after us, like every mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much so. Also to my father. The whole family was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother help out your father in the tailoring business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had how many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been seven together. Like four brothers-- sorry. Two sisters and five brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the oldest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nojech.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he known as Jacobs or Jakubowicz previous to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakubowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was Nojech born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1905, November the first. Something like this, November, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your next sibling, your next brother or sister was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest sister was Cluwa. You know, her name was Cluwa. Like here, she would be called Celine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her surname, her family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakubowicz also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boguchwal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year, approximately, would she have been born?

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the oldest of all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was Cluwa, Nojech--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naftula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would have been born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- 1915, I think, if I'm correct. Yeah, it would be 1915, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after Naftula, came who?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Berju. Berju.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when would Berju have been born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born in 1916. 1915, 1916-- it doesn't sound-- I know he was two years older than me. So Naftula must have been born in 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was next?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Berju?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after you came who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaskl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Born approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after Chaskl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Chuma. Is Chuma. She is in Toronto too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born when?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you closest to, amongst your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very, very close to each other, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways was that closeness demonstrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a close family, very close. We lived together, and we stayed together. And then, when my older brother got married, we were very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had said that your oldest sister was married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cluwa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do recall her wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children did she have?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had three boys and one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Leizer. One was Gutman. And one was Moti. And the girl's name I don't remember. She was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how old were they in 1940 or when the war broke out, 1939? Approximately how old were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl was about two years old. And then the other ones were five, eight, and one was 13 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their family name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Boguchwal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family home. How many rooms did your family have? What was the atmosphere? What types of books might I have seen in it? Just give a description of what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one, two, three, four, five rooms. And then we a at separate room. Because we had our own house, with tenants, too. But not in our apartment, in separate apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like it was dining room, three bedrooms, and then he had another room, a separate for me and my brother. And we had a kitchen. Like a real family house. Who did you share a room with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Chaskl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever cause any problems between you and him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would I have seen, if I would have come into your bedroom, besides the beds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. I would say, I would say the beds. Maybe there did stay something there, the books or whatever it is. It wasn't like in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The books that I would have seen. What books might they have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was a very-- the younger-- he was very, very good in school. I remember he was three years a tailor. And then he said, I want to be somebody and started to go to high school. And he was a very, very good student, very ambitious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaskl, my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he decided he wanted to go back to school, was your father in agreement with that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no question about it. I remember he said, all right, the rent what that tenant is paying would be paid for in the school for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many tenants did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had 36 tenants.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that all in one building?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were four buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they like apartment buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one was an apartment. They did call in the old country like apartments. But they were houses, let's say. There were eight rooms in one apartment, meant like a room in a kitchen, like eight apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in addition to being a tailor, your father was a landlord as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As tenants, did you have family members? Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There used to live there my mother's brother in one apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michlewicz, Avrum Michlewicz. Then my older sister lived also in our apartment. And then my grandparents from my father's side, they became older, and my father brought them over and gave them an apartment. And they lived also there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a typical Shabbos at your family's home before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After coming home from the shul, like from the synagogue- it was not a synagogue. It was like-- in the old country they said a one room to describe like a shtiebel, that's what it was called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which translates as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say a small, very small, synagogue. And we came home. And we ate-- I remember I used to go to the bakery next door. And my mother made on Friday a cholent, if you know what that means, a cholent. And we had to go there and bring it home. If we wouldn't go on time, somebody else would have taken it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to go the night before to take that to the bakery? Why couldn't your mother just make it at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was the custom. It had to stay overnight, a cholent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it couldn't stay in her oven? It had to stay in the bakery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the baker made the oven. And in the old country, it wasn't like you had all the kind of conveniences that you have here. Now you put it on an electric stove, and it has to stay over night. But there it wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have anything to do with it being Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you couldn't turn off electricity, or you couldn't turn it on, the electricity. Well, it was a custom like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have a bar mitzvah. I had only what they called to the-- to [INAUDIBLE] and read my-- whatever I had to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your part.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did have the synagogue component of a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I didn't have at home like to make a supper or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a kaddish at the shtiebel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There was a kaddish, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody had a drink. Whatever that was, I don't remember. Maybe it was wine and some cookies. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall being excited in anticipation of coming to bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, in a way. I didn't know it. To be honest, I didn't know very much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the community that you grew up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Tomaszow was a very nice, beautiful city, well, a small town. There were between 15,000 and 16,000 Jewish people living in Tomaszow. And it was a very beautiful, young generation, very beautiful. It's very-- it's so hard to describe when you talk about this. We'd always been happy, very happy. And we always, like I said, we went as young people. Well, after I would say about 14, 15 years, and he met together. We had some very nice organizations, like [INAUDIBLE] and Hagibor, all kinds of Zionist organizations. And we spent a lot of time-- like I have some time after school, Saturday mostly. And we met together, at night, sometimes until about 9, 10 o'clock. And then we had to go home. But the young boys and girls. Everybody is saying this. But I think we had the nicest young boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made mention of a club that you belonged to, an organization. And you had to go home at 9:00, 10:00. What organization was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just sport. We played soccer, we played ping pong. We had-- had it called the JTGS. The most time I spend there. And all the young boys and girls there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the complete name of that organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called-- in Polish it's called [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which translates as?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish Organization Sport Fed-- that's what I did translate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your best sport?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did play everything. I even played basketball. The best, what I was very, very good, was ping-pong.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you played soccer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I played soccer, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play in an organized team?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. JTGS. And we had our own soccer club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you play against?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we played a lot of teams. We had one German team was called Victoria. And then there was [INAUDIBLE] Then there was [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was like to say that was a club like the NDP, was called [INAUDIBLE]. And then was Hakoah, and one was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism in the course of your competition against Gentile clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much so. If they could knock your feet, they did it. And if we won, they could fight after the game with us too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall incidents of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall that one time was played [INAUDIBLE] and from Lodz, a club called [INAUDIBLE]. And they did play for a higher-- like there was divided A, B, C, and to go in a higher category. And at that time, the [INAUDIBLE] won. And that was a very, very bad that time, the fighting, after the game.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to just stop there. We're going to continue on the next tape.
